user_message: [IMAGE ATTACHED - you MUST call vision_analyze immediately] my tomato plant has dark spots

inputs.user_description: my tomato plant has dark spots

output.visual_confidence_note: The characteristic patterns and affected plant type match Early Blight.

output.query: treatment protocol and care information for Early Blight affecting Tomato plant

results.content:
Disease: Tomato Early Blight

Affects: Tomato, Potato, Eggplant

Symptoms: Dark brown to black lesions with concentric rings forming a bullseye pattern. Lesions typically appear on older lower leaves first. Surrounding tissue turns yellow. Severely affected leaves drop prematurely.

Causes: Fungal pathogen Alternaria solani. Spreads via wind and water splash. Favored by warm temperatures (24-29°C), high humidity, and wet foliage.

Treatment:
Remove and destroy all affected leaves immediately
Apply copper-based fungicide (copper hydroxide or copper sulfate) every 7-10 days
Alternatively use chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicides
Avoid overhead irrigation — water at base of plant
Apply mulch to prevent soil splash onto lower leaves

Prevention: Rotate crops every 2-3 years. Use certified disease-free seed. Space plants adequately for airflow. Remove plant debris after harvest.

Severity: HIGH — can cause significant defoliation and yield loss if untreated. Act within 48 hours of first symptoms.

Disease: Tomato Late Blight

Affects: Tomato, Potato, Petunia

Symptoms: Water-soaked, dark green to lead-gray spots on leaves that expand rapidly into large brown lesions. White, fuzzy fungal growth often appears on the undersides of leaves in humid conditions. Stems develop dark brown streaks.

Causes: Oomycete pathogen Phytophthora infestans. Spreads rapidly through wind-borne sporangia. Favored by cool, moist nights (10-15°C) and moderate day temperatures (15-21°C).

Treatment:
Prune and destroy infected plants immediately; do not compost
Apply fungicides containing chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or fixed copper
Use biofungicides containing Bacillus subtilis for organic control
Ensure foliage stays dry by improving ventilation

Prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid planting tomatoes near potatoes. Destroy volunteer plants and cull piles. Use drip irrigation.

Severity: CRITICAL — can destroy an entire crop within days under favorable conditions.

Disease: Potato Early Blight

Affects: Potato, Tomato, Eggplant

Symptoms: Small, dark, dry papery spots on older leaves. As spots enlarge, they show characteristic concentric "bullseye" rings. Tubers may develop dark, sunken, circular lesions with corky tissue underneath.

Causes: Fungus Alternaria solani. Spreads via wind and water splash. Favored by alternating wet and dry periods.

Treatment:
Apply fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin)
Ensure plants have adequate nitrogen and potassium to maintain vigor
Harvest tubers only when vines are completely dead to reduce skin damage

Prevention: Practice 3-year crop rotation. Use certified disease-free seed tubers. Avoid overhead irrigation. Control volunteer potato plants.

Severity: MODERATE to HIGH — reduces tuber size and storage life.
